question: \text { A-4. Of all ellipses inscribed in a square, show that the circle has the maximum perimeter. }
solution: A-4 Let the square of sidelength \( 2 R \) have the vertices \( ( \pm R \sqrt{2}, 0) \) and \( (0, \pm R, \overline{2}) \). The ellipse \[ \frac{x^{\llcorner }}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{\llcorner }}{b^{2}}=1 \] with \( 0 \leqq b \leqq a \leqq R \sqrt{2} \) has the line \( x+y=R \sqrt{2} \) as a tangent if and only if the quadratic equation \( x^{2} / a^{2}+(R \sqrt{2}-x)^{2} / b^{2}=1 \) has a double root. It can be verified that its discriminant vanishes if and only if \( a^{2}+b^{2}=2 R^{2} \). As \( a \) varies from \( R \) to \( R \sqrt{2} \) and \( b \) varies from \( R \) to 0 , the curve (1) varies from the circle of radius \( R \) through all the non-circular ellipses inscribed in the square to the degenerate "flat" ellipse lying on the \( x \)-axis. Let \( 4 L \) denote the length of the ellipse \( x=a \cos t, y=b \sin t, 0 \leqq t \leqq 2 \pi \). Then \[ \begin{array}{c} L=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[a^{2} \sin ^{2} t+b^{2} \cos ^{2} t\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d t=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} a^{2}(1-\cos 2 t)+\frac{1}{2} b^{2}(1+\cos 2 t)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d t \\ =\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[R^{2}-\frac{1}{2} c^{2} \cos 2 t\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d t \end{array} \] where \( c^{2}=a^{2}-b^{2} \). The last integral we split into one from 0 to \( \pi / 4 \) and one from \( \pi / 4 \) to \( \pi / 2 \), and in the latter we substitute \( t=\pi / 2-t^{\prime} \), obtaining \[ L=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left\{\left[R^{2}-\frac{1}{2} c^{2} \cos 2 t\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}+\left[R^{2}+\frac{1}{2} c^{2} \cos 2 t\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\right\} d t \] Note that \( \cos 2 t>0 \) for \( 0 \leqq t<\pi / 4 \). Now the function \( f(u)=(p-u)^{\frac{1}{2}}+(p+u)^{\frac{1}{2}} \) decreases in the interval \( 0 \leqq u \leqq p \), because \( 2 f^{\prime}(u)=-(p-u)^{-\frac{1}{2}}+(p+u)^{-\frac{1}{2}}<0 \) for \( 00 \] Taking the "highest," "lowest," "rightest," and 'leftest'" points on the ellipse, we see that all four sides of the square must be tangents to the ellipse. The line \( u=R \) is a tangent if and only if the equation \( C v^{2}+(B R+E) v \) \( +\left(A r^{2}+D r+F\right)=0 \) has a double root or \[ (B R+E)^{2}-4 C\left(A R^{2}+D R+F\right)=0 \] The corresponding conditions for \( u=-R, v=R \) and \( v=-R \) are \[ (-B R+E)^{2}-4 C\left(A R^{2}-D R+F\right)=0 \] \[ (B R+D)^{2}-4 A\left(C R^{2}+E R+F\right)=0 \] respectively. Subtract (2) from (3) and divide by \( 4 R \); this gives \[ 2 C D-B E=0 \] Similarly, from (4) and (5), \[ -B D+2 A E=0 \] By (6), (7) and (1), \( D=E=0 \). Therefore (2) and (4) become \[ B^{2} R^{2}-4 A C R^{2}-4 C F=0, B^{2} R^{2}-4 A C R^{2}-4 A F=0 \] respectively. Since \( F \neq 0 \), we have \( A=C \); this means that the ellipse has its axes along the lines \( u \pm v=0 \).

descriptive_long.question: \text { A-4. Of all ellipses inscribed in a square, show that the circle has the maximum perimeter. }
descriptive_long.solution:
A-4 Let the square of sidelength \( 2 halfsidelg \) have the vertices \( ( \pm halfsidelg \sqrt{2}, 0) \) and \( (0, \pm halfsidelg, \overline{2}) \). The ellipse
\[
\frac{horizcoor^{\llcorner }}{semimajor^{2}}+\frac{verticoor^{\llcorner }}{semiminor^{2}}=1
\]
with \( 0 \leqq semiminor \leqq semimajor \leqq halfsidelg \sqrt{2} \) has the line \( horizcoor+verticoor=halfsidelg \sqrt{2} \) as a tangent if and only if the quadratic equation \( horizcoor^{2} / semimajor^{2}+(halfsidelg \sqrt{2}-horizcoor)^{2} / semiminor^{2}=1 \) has a double root. It can be verified that its discriminant vanishes if and only if \( semimajor^{2}+semiminor^{2}=2 halfsidelg^{2} \). As \( semimajor \) varies from \( halfsidelg \) to \( halfsidelg \sqrt{2} \) and \( semiminor \) varies from \( halfsidelg \) to 0 , the curve (1) varies from the circle of radius \( halfsidelg \) through all the non-circular ellipses inscribed in the square to the degenerate "flat" ellipse lying on the \( horizcoor \)-axis.

Let \( 4 arclength \) denote the length of the ellipse \( horizcoor=semimajor \cos paramangl, verticoor=semiminor \sin paramangl, 0 \leqq paramangl \leqq 2 \pi \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
arclength=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[semimajor^{2} \sin ^{2} paramangl+semiminor^{2} \cos ^{2} paramangl\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d paramangl=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} semimajor^{2}(1-\cos 2 paramangl)+\frac{1}{2} semiminor^{2}(1+\cos 2 paramangl)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d paramangl \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[halfsidelg^{2}-\frac{1}{2} focalparam^{2} \cos 2 paramangl\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d paramangl
\end{array}
\]
where \( focalparam^{2}=semimajor^{2}-semiminor^{2} \). The last integral we split into one from 0 to \( \pi / 4 \) and one from \( \pi / 4 \) to \( \pi / 2 \), and in the latter we substitute \( paramangl=\pi / 2-paramangl^{\prime} \), obtaining
\[
arclength=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left\{\left[halfsidelg^{2}-\frac{1}{2} focalparam^{2} \cos 2 paramangl\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}+\left[halfsidelg^{2}+\frac{1}{2} focalparam^{2} \cos 2 paramangl\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\right\} d paramangl
\]

Note that \( \cos 2 paramangl>0 \) for \( 0 \leqq paramangl<\pi / 4 \).
Now the function \( funcexpr(tiltedhori)=(midvalue-tiltedhori)^{\frac{1}{2}}+(midvalue+tiltedhori)^{\frac{1}{2}} \) decreases in the interval \( 0 \leqq tiltedhori \leqq midvalue \), because \( 2 funcexpr^{\prime}(tiltedhori)=-(midvalue-tiltedhori)^{-\frac{1}{2}}+(midvalue+tiltedhori)^{-\frac{1}{2}}<0 \) for \( 0<tiltedhori<midvalue \). Thus the integral in (2) as a function of \( focalparam \) has its largest value when \( focalparam=0 \), that is, for the inscribed circle.

To show that an ellipse inscribed in the square must have its axes along the diagonals of the square, we choose the square as having sides \( tiltedhori= \pm halfsidelg \) and \( tiltedvert= \pm halfsidelg \) and the ellipse as having the equation
\[
coeffalpha\, tiltedhori^{2}+coeffbravo\, tiltedhori\, tiltedvert+coeffcharlie\, tiltedvert^{2}+coeffdelta\, tiltedhori+coeffecho\, tiltedvert+coefffoxtrot=0
\]
where
\[
4\, coeffalpha\, coeffcharlie-coeffbravo^{2}>0
\]

Taking the "highest," "lowest," "rightest," and "leftest" points on the ellipse, we see that all four sides of the square must be tangents to the ellipse.

The line \( tiltedhori=halfsidelg \) is a tangent if and only if the equation \( coeffcharlie\, tiltedvert^{2}+(coeffbravo\, halfsidelg+coeffecho)\, tiltedvert +\left(coeffalpha\, radiusvar^{2}+coeffdelta\, radiusvar+coefffoxtrot\right)=0 \) has a double root or
\[
(coeffbravo\, halfsidelg+coeffecho)^{2}-4\, coeffcharlie\left(coeffalpha\, halfsidelg^{2}+coeffdelta\, halfsidelg+coefffoxtrot\right)=0
\]

The corresponding conditions for \( tiltedhori=-halfsidelg, tiltedvert=halfsidelg \) and \( tiltedvert=-halfsidelg \) are
\[
(-coeffbravo\, halfsidelg+coeffecho)^{2}-4\, coeffcharlie\left(coeffalpha\, halfsidelg^{2}-coeffdelta\, halfsidelg+coefffoxtrot\right)=0
\]
\[
(coeffbravo\, halfsidelg+coeffdelta)^{2}-4\, coeffalpha\left(coeffcharlie\, halfsidelg^{2}+coeffecho\, halfsidelg+coefffoxtrot\right)=0
\]
respectively. Subtract (2) from (3) and divide by \( 4 halfsidelg \); this gives
\[
2\, coeffcharlie\, coeffdelta-coeffbravo\, coeffecho=0
\]

Similarly, from (4) and (5),
\[
-coeffbravo\, coeffdelta+2\, coeffalpha\, coeffecho=0
\]

By (6), (7) and (1), \( coeffdelta=coeffecho=0 \). Therefore (2) and (4) become
\[
coeffbravo^{2}\, halfsidelg^{2}-4\, coeffalpha\, coeffcharlie\, halfsidelg^{2}-4\, coeffcharlie\, coefffoxtrot=0,\quad coeffbravo^{2}\, halfsidelg^{2}-4\, coeffalpha\, coeffcharlie\, halfsidelg^{2}-4\, coeffalpha\, coefffoxtrot=0
\]
respectively. Since \( coefffoxtrot \neq 0 \), we have \( coeffalpha=coeffcharlie \); this means that the ellipse has its axes along the lines \( tiltedhori \pm tiltedvert=0 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: \text { A-4. Of all ellipses inscribed in a square, show that the circle has the maximum perimeter. }
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-4 Let the square of sidelength \( 2 carpentry \) have the vertices \( ( \pm carpentry \sqrt{2}, 0) \) and \( (0, \pm carpentry, \overline{2}) \). The ellipse
\[
\frac{sandstone^{\llcorner }}{ploughshare^{2}}+\frac{driftwood^{\llcorner }}{cantilever^{2}}=1
\]
with \( 0 \leqq cantilever \leqq ploughshare \leqq carpentry \sqrt{2} \) has the line \( sandstone+driftwood=carpentry \sqrt{2} \) as a tangent if and only if the quadratic equation \( sandstone^{2} / ploughshare^{2}+(carpentry \sqrt{2}-sandstone)^{2} / cantilever^{2}=1 \) has a double root. It can be verified that its discriminant vanishes if and only if \( ploughshare^{2}+cantilever^{2}=2 carpentry^{2} \). As \( ploughshare \) varies from \( carpentry \) to \( carpentry \sqrt{2} \) and \( cantilever \) varies from \( carpentry \) to 0 , the curve (1) varies from the circle of radius \( carpentry \) through all the non-circular ellipses inscribed in the square to the degenerate "flat" ellipse lying on the \( sandstone \)-axis.

Let \( 4 salamander \) denote the length of the ellipse \( sandstone=ploughshare \cos trelliswork, driftwood=cantilever \sin trelliswork, 0 \leqq trelliswork \leqq 2 \pi \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
salamander=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[ploughshare^{2} \sin ^{2} trelliswork+cantilever^{2} \cos ^{2} trelliswork\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d\,trelliswork=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} ploughshare^{2}(1-\cos 2 trelliswork)+\frac{1}{2} cantilever^{2}(1+\cos 2 trelliswork)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d\,trelliswork \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[carpentry^{2}-\frac{1}{2} parchment^{2} \cos 2 trelliswork\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d\,trelliswork
\end{array}
\]
where \( parchment^{2}=ploughshare^{2}-cantilever^{2} \). The last integral we split into one from 0 to \( \pi / 4 \) and one from \( \pi / 4 \) to \( \pi / 2 \), and in the latter we substitute \( trelliswork=\pi / 2-trelliswork^{\prime} \), obtaining
\[
salamander=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left\{\left[carpentry^{2}-\frac{1}{2} parchment^{2} \cos 2 trelliswork\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}+\left[carpentry^{2}+\frac{1}{2} parchment^{2} \cos 2 trelliswork\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\right\} d\,trelliswork
\]

Note that \( \cos 2 trelliswork>0 \) for \( 0 \leqq trelliswork<\pi / 4 \).
Now the function \( candlewick(buttercup)=(goldsmith-buttercup)^{\frac{1}{2}}+(goldsmith+buttercup)^{\frac{1}{2}} \) decreases in the interval \( 0 \leqq buttercup \leqq goldsmith \), because \( 2 candlewick^{\prime}(buttercup)=-(goldsmith-buttercup)^{-\frac{1}{2}}+(goldsmith+buttercup)^{-\frac{1}{2}}<0 \) for \( 0<buttercup<goldsmith \). Thus the integral in (2) as a function of \( parchment \) has its largest value when \( parchment=0 \), that is, for the inscribed circle.

To show that an ellipse inscribed in the square must have its axes along the diagonals of the square, we choose the square as having sides \( buttercup= \pm carpentry \) and \( dragonfly= \pm carpentry \) and the ellipse as having the equation
\[
blueberry buttercup^{2}+rainstorm buttercup dragonfly+lighthouse dragonfly^{2}+quesadilla buttercup+marshmallow dragonfly+blacksmith=0
\]
where
\[
4 blueberry lighthouse-rainstorm^{2}>0
\]

Taking the "highest," "lowest," "rightest," and 'leftest'" points on the ellipse, we see that all four sides of the square must be tangents to the ellipse.

The line \( buttercup=carpentry \) is a tangent if and only if the equation \( lighthouse dragonfly^{2}+(rainstorm carpentry+marshmallow) dragonfly +\left(blueberry rainwater^{2}+quesadilla rainwater+blacksmith\right)=0 \) has a double root or
\[
(rainstorm carpentry+marshmallow)^{2}-4 lighthouse\left(blueberry carpentry^{2}+quesadilla carpentry+blacksmith\right)=0
\]

The corresponding conditions for \( buttercup=-carpentry, dragonfly=carpentry \) and \( dragonfly=-carpentry \) are
\[
(-rainstorm carpentry+marshmallow)^{2}-4 lighthouse\left(blueberry carpentry^{2}-quesadilla carpentry+blacksmith\right)=0
\]
\[
(rainstorm carpentry+quesadilla)^{2}-4 blueberry\left(lighthouse carpentry^{2}+marshmallow carpentry+blacksmith\right)=0
\]
respectively. Subtract (2) from (3) and divide by \( 4 carpentry \); this gives
\[
2 lighthouse quesadilla-rainstorm marshmallow=0
\]

Similarly, from (4) and (5),
\[
-rainstorm quesadilla+2 blueberry marshmallow=0
\]

By (6), (7) and (1), \( quesadilla=marshmallow=0 \). Therefore (2) and (4) become
\[
rainstorm^{2} carpentry^{2}-4 blueberry lighthouse carpentry^{2}-4 lighthouse blacksmith=0, \; rainstorm^{2} carpentry^{2}-4 blueberry lighthouse carpentry^{2}-4 blueberry blacksmith=0
\]
respectively. Since \( blacksmith \neq 0 \), we have \( blueberry=lighthouse \); this means that the ellipse has its axes along the lines \( buttercup \pm dragonfly=0 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: \text { A-4. Of all ellipses inscribed in a square, show that the circle has the maximum perimeter. }
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-4 Let the square of sidelength \( 2 thinness \) have the vertices \( ( \pm thinness \sqrt{2}, 0) \) and \((0, \pm thinness, \overline{2})\). The ellipse
\[
\frac{verticalpo^{\llcorner }}{concaveax^{2}}+\frac{horizontal^{\llcorner }}{convexax^{2}}=1
\]
with \( 0 \leqq convexax \leqq concaveax \leqq thinness \sqrt{2} \) has the line \( verticalpo+horizontal=thinness \sqrt{2} \) as a tangent if and only if the quadratic equation \( verticalpo^{2} / concaveax^{2}+(thinness \sqrt{2}-verticalpo)^{2} / convexax^{2}=1 \) has a double root. It can be verified that its discriminant vanishes if and only if \( concaveax^{2}+convexax^{2}=2 thinness^{2} \). As \( concaveax \) varies from \( thinness \) to \( thinness \sqrt{2} \) and \( convexax \) varies from \( thinness \) to 0, the curve (1) varies from the circle of radius \( thinness \) through all the non-circular ellipses inscribed in the square to the degenerate "flat" ellipse lying on the \( verticalpo \)-axis.

Let \( 4 shortness \) denote the length of the ellipse \( verticalpo=concaveax \cos stillness, \; horizontal=convexax \sin stillness, \; 0 \leqq stillness \leqq 2 \pi \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
shortness=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[concaveax^{2} \sin ^{2} stillness+convexax^{2} \cos ^{2} stillness\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d stillness=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} concaveax^{2}(1-\cos 2 stillness)+\frac{1}{2} convexax^{2}(1+\cos 2 stillness)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d stillness \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[thinness^{2}-\frac{1}{2} converged^{2} \cos 2 stillness\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d stillness
\end{array}
\]
where \( converged^{2}=concaveax^{2}-convexax^{2} \). The last integral we split into one from 0 to \( \pi / 4 \) and one from \( \pi / 4 \) to \( \pi / 2 \), and in the latter we substitute \( stillness=\pi / 2-stillness^{\prime} \), obtaining
\[
shortness=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left\{\left[thinness^{2}-\frac{1}{2} converged^{2} \cos 2 stillness\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}+\left[thinness^{2}+\frac{1}{2} converged^{2} \cos 2 stillness\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\right\} d stillness
\]

Note that \( \cos 2 stillness>0 \) for \( 0 \leqq stillness<\pi / 4 \).
Now the function \( rigidity(constants)=(variance-constants)^{\frac{1}{2}}+(variance+constants)^{\frac{1}{2}} \) decreases in the interval \( 0 \leqq constants \leqq variance \), because \( 2 rigidity^{\prime}(constants)=-(variance-constants)^{-\frac{1}{2}}+(variance+constants)^{-\frac{1}{2}}<0 \) for \( 0<constants<variance \). Thus the integral in (2) as a function of \( converged \) has its largest value when \( converged=0 \), that is, for the inscribed circle.

To show that an ellipse inscribed in the square must have its axes along the diagonals of the square, we choose the square as having sides \( constants= \pm thinness \) and \( steadfast= \pm thinness \) and the ellipse as having the equation
\[
independent\,constants^{2}+unrelated\,constants\,steadfast+unaffected\,steadfast^{2}+staticity\,constants+instabile\,steadfast+variablex=0
\]
where
\[
4\,independent\,unaffected-unrelated^{2}>0
\]

Taking the "highest," "lowest," "rightest," and 'leftest'" points on the ellipse, we see that all four sides of the square must be tangents to the ellipse.

The line \( constants=thinness \) is a tangent if and only if the equation \( unaffected\,steadfast^{2}+(unrelated\,thinness+instabile)\,steadfast +(independent\,edgeless^{2}+staticity\,edgeless+variablex)=0 \) has a double root or
\[
(unrelated\,thinness+instabile)^{2}-4\,unaffected\left(independent\,thinness^{2}+staticity\,thinness+variablex\right)=0
\]

The corresponding conditions for \( constants=-thinness, \; steadfast=thinness \) and \( steadfast=-thinness \) are
\[
(-unrelated\,thinness+instabile)^{2}-4\,unaffected\left(independent\,thinness^{2}-staticity\,thinness+variablex\right)=0
\]
\[
(unrelated\,thinness+staticity)^{2}-4\,independent\left(unaffected\,thinness^{2}+instabile\,thinness+variablex\right)=0
\]
respectively. Subtract (2) from (3) and divide by \( 4 thinness \); this gives
\[
2\,unaffected\,staticity-unrelated\,instabile=0
\]

Similarly, from (4) and (5),
\[
-unrelated\,staticity+2\,independent\,instabile=0
\]

By (6), (7) and (1), \( staticity=instabile=0 \). Therefore (2) and (4) become
\[
unrelated^{2} thinness^{2}-4\,independent\,unaffected\,thinness^{2}-4\,unaffected\,variablex=0, \quad unrelated^{2} thinness^{2}-4\,independent\,unaffected\,thinness^{2}-4\,independent\,variablex=0
\]
respectively. Since \( variablex \neq 0 \), we have \( independent=unaffected \); this means that the ellipse has its axes along the lines \( constants \pm steadfast=0 \).

garbled_string.question: \text { A-4. Of all ellipses inscribed in a square, show that the circle has the maximum perimeter. }
garbled_string.solution:
A-4 Let the square of sidelength \( 2 nskgjqlwe \) have the vertices \( ( \pm nskgjqlwe \sqrt{2}, 0) \) and \( (0, \pm nskgjqlwe, \overline{2}) \). The ellipse
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp^{\llcorner }}{jratvchbo^{2}}+\frac{hjgrksla^{\llcorner }}{gxdmruqsz^{2}}=1
\]
with \( 0 \leqq gxdmruqsz \leqq jratvchbo \leqq nskgjqlwe \sqrt{2} \) has the line \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla=nskgjqlwe \sqrt{2} \) as a tangent if and only if the quadratic equation \( qzxwvtnp^{2} / jratvchbo^{2}+(nskgjqlwe \sqrt{2}-qzxwvtnp)^{2} / gxdmruqsz^{2}=1 \) has a double root. It can be verified that its discriminant vanishes if and only if \( jratvchbo^{2}+gxdmruqsz^{2}=2 nskgjqlwe^{2} \). As \( jratvchbo \) varies from \( nskgjqlwe \) to \( nskgjqlwe \sqrt{2} \) and \( gxdmruqsz \) varies from \( nskgjqlwe \) to 0 , the curve (1) varies from the circle of radius \( nskgjqlwe \) through all the non-circular ellipses inscribed in the square to the degenerate "flat" ellipse lying on the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis.

Let \( 4 rmcfujbka \) denote the length of the ellipse \( qzxwvtnp=jratvchbo \cos mndpvcia, hjgrksla=gxdmruqsz \sin mndpvcia, 0 \leqq mndpvcia \leqq 2 \pi \). Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
rmcfujbka=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[jratvchbo^{2} \sin ^{2} mndpvcia+gxdmruqsz^{2} \cos ^{2} mndpvcia\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d mndpvcia=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[\frac{1}{2} jratvchbo^{2}(1-\cos 2 mndpvcia)+\frac{1}{2} gxdmruqsz^{2}(1+\cos 2 mndpvcia)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d mndpvcia \\
=\int_{0}^{\pi / 2}\left[nskgjqlwe^{2}-\frac{1}{2} wplkzenya^{2} \cos 2 mndpvcia\right]^{\frac{1}{2}} d mndpvcia
\end{array}
\]
where \( wplkzenya^{2}=jratvchbo^{2}-gxdmruqsz^{2} \). The last integral we split into one from 0 to \( \pi / 4 \) and one from \( \pi / 4 \) to \( \pi / 2 \), and in the latter we substitute \( mndpvcia=\pi / 2-mndpvcia^{\prime} \), obtaining
\[
rmcfujbka=\int_{0}^{\pi / 4}\left\{\left[nskgjqlwe^{2}-\frac{1}{2} wplkzenya^{2} \cos 2 mndpvcia\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}+\left[nskgjqlwe^{2}+\frac{1}{2} wplkzenya^{2} \cos 2 mndpvcia\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\right\} d mndpvcia
\]

Note that \( \cos 2 mndpvcia>0 \) for \( 0 \leqq mndpvcia<\pi / 4 \).
Now the function \( osiuxlqen(ksbxqjre)=(htzkncavo-ksbxqjre)^{\frac{1}{2}}+(htzkncavo+ksbxqjre)^{\frac{1}{2}} \) decreases in the interval \( 0 \leqq ksbxqjre \leqq htzkncavo \), because \( 2 osiuxlqen^{\prime}(ksbxqjre)=-(htzkncavo-ksbxqjre)^{-\frac{1}{2}}+(htzkncavo+ksbxqjre)^{-\frac{1}{2}}<0 \) for \( 0<ksbxqjre<htzkncavo \). Thus the integral in (2) as a function of \( wplkzenya \) has its largest value when \( wplkzenya=0 \), that is, for the inscribed circle.

To show that an ellipse inscribed in the square must have its axes along the diagonals of the square, we choose the square as having sides \( ksbxqjre= \pm nskgjqlwe \) and \( lthwymod= \pm nskgjqlwe \) and the ellipse as having the equation
\[
zrfvopkia ksbxqjre^{2}+glsemydtu ksbxqjre lthwymod+bnqazxwer lthwymod^{2}+lmvkpstio ksbxqjre+xtqwhsarc lthwymod+pdohlwgye=0
\]
where
\[
4 zrfvopkia bnqazxwer-glsemydtu^{2}>0
\]

Taking the "highest," "lowest," "rightest," and 'leftest'" points on the ellipse, we see that all four sides of the square must be tangents to the ellipse.

The line \( ksbxqjre=nskgjqlwe \) is a tangent if and only if the equation \( bnqazxwer lthwymod^{2}+(glsemydtu nskgjqlwe+xtqwhsarc) lthwymod+\left(zrfvopkia yfgpdsmci^{2}+lmvkpstio yfgpdsmci+pdohlwgye\right)=0 \) has a double root or
\[
(glsemydtu nskgjqlwe+xtqwhsarc)^{2}-4 bnqazxwer\left(zrfvopkia nskgjqlwe^{2}+lmvkpstio nskgjqlwe+pdohlwgye\right)=0
\]

The corresponding conditions for \( ksbxqjre=-nskgjqlwe, lthwymod=nskgjqlwe \) and \( lthwymod=-nskgjqlwe \) are
\[
(-glsemydtu nskgjqlwe+xtqwhsarc)^{2}-4 bnqazxwer\left(zrfvopkia nskgjqlwe^{2}-lmvkpstio nskgjqlwe+pdohlwgye\right)=0
\]
\[
(glsemydtu nskgjqlwe+lmvkpstio)^{2}-4 zrfvopkia\left(bnqazxwer nskgjqlwe^{2}+xtqwhsarc nskgjqlwe+pdohlwgye\right)=0
\]
respectively. Subtract (2) from (3) and divide by \( 4 nskgjqlwe \); this gives
\[
2 bnqazxwer lmvkpstio-glsemydtu xtqwhsarc=0
\]

Similarly, from (4) and (5),
\[
-glsemydtu lmvkpstio+2 zrfvopkia xtqwhsarc=0
\]

By (6), (7) and (1), \( lmvkpstio=xtqwhsarc=0 \). Therefore (2) and (4) become
\[
glsemydtu^{2} nskgjqlwe^{2}-4 zrfvopkia bnqazxwer nskgjqlwe^{2}-4 bnqazxwer pdohlwgye=0,\quad glsemydtu^{2} nskgjqlwe^{2}-4 zrfvopkia bnqazxwer nskgjqlwe^{2}-4 zrfvopkia pdohlwgye=0
\]
respectively. Since \( pdohlwgye \neq 0 \), we have \( zrfvopkia=bnqazxwer \); this means that the ellipse has its axes along the lines \( ksbxqjre \pm lthwymod=0 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer and set  
\[
\mathcal C_{n}:=\bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})\in\mathbb R^{n}\;:\;|x_{i}|\le 1\text{ for }1\le i\le n\bigr\},
\]
the closed unit $n$-cube centred at the origin.

For every invertible matrix $A\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times n}$ define the ellipsoid  
\[
\mathcal E(A):=\bigl\{Au\;:\;u\in\mathbb R^{n},\ \lVert u\rVert_{2}\le 1\bigr\} \tag{1}
\]
and denote by $S(A)$ the $(n-1)$-dimensional Hausdorff measure of $\partial\mathcal E(A)$.

The ellipsoid $\mathcal E(A)$ is called \emph{admissible} if  

(i) $\mathcal E(A)\subset\mathcal C_{n}$, and  

(ii) $\mathcal E(A)$ is tangent to each of the $2n$ facets $x_{i}=\pm 1$ of $\mathcal C_{n}$  
(the inclusion in (i) follows from (ii), but we state both for emphasis).

a) Prove the isoperimetric-type inequality  
\[
S(A)\le S(I_{n})=\lvert S^{\,n-1}\rvert , \tag{2}
\]
for every admissible matrix $A$.

b) Show that equality in \textup{(2)} holds if and only if $A$ is orthogonal, i.e. $AA^{\top}=I_{n}$.  
Hence, among all ellipsoids that are contained in the cube and tangent to every facet, the Euclidean unit ball is the unique (up to rotation) maximiser of the surface area.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
G:=AA^{\top}\in\mathbb R^{\,n\times n},\qquad G\text{ symmetric positive-definite}. \tag{3}
\]
Then  
\[
\mathcal E(A)=\bigl\{x\in\mathbb R^{n}\;:\;x^{\top}G^{-1}x\le 1\bigr\}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 0.  Containment from tangency via support functions.  
For a convex body $K$ the support function is $h_{K}(\xi):=\sup_{x\in K}x\cdot\xi$.
For the cube one has  
\[
h_{\mathcal C_{n}}(\xi)=\sum_{i=1}^{n}|\,\xi_{i}\,|. \tag{5}
\]
For the ellipsoid (4) the support function equals  
\[
h_{\mathcal E(A)}(\xi)=\sqrt{\xi^{\top}G\,\xi}. \tag{6}
\]
Because of tangency we have $h_{\mathcal E(A)}(\pm e_{i})=1$ for every $i$.  
Now $\pm e_{i}$ are the extreme points of the cross-polytope  
\(
\bigl\{\xi\in\mathbb R^{n}\;:\;h_{\mathcal C_{n}}(\xi)=1\bigr\},
\)
and both $h_{\mathcal E(A)}$ and $h_{\mathcal C_{n}}$ are convex and positively
homogeneous of degree $1$.  Therefore  
\[
h_{\mathcal E(A)}(\xi)\le h_{\mathcal C_{n}}(\xi)\quad\forall\,\xi\in\mathbb R^{n},
\]
which is equivalent to $\mathcal E(A)\subset\mathcal C_{n}$.

Step 1.  Tangency forces unit diagonal.  
Fix $k\in\{1,\dots ,n\}$ and maximise $e_{k}\cdot x$ subject to $x^{\top}G^{-1}x=1$.
A Lagrange multiplier calculation yields the maximiser  
\[
x^{\ast}=\frac{Ge_{k}}{\sqrt{e_{k}^{\top}Ge_{k}}},\qquad
\max=\sqrt{e_{k}^{\top}Ge_{k}}.
\]
Because the facet $x_{k}=1$ is tangent, the maximum equals $1$, hence  
\[
G_{kk}=1\qquad(1\le k\le n). \tag{7}
\]

Step 2.  Surface area of $\mathcal E(A)$.  
Let $\sigma$ denote the surface measure on $S^{\,n-1}$.
The smooth map  
\[
F:S^{\,n-1}\longrightarrow\partial\mathcal E(A),\qquad F(u)=Au, \tag{8}
\]
has $(n-1)$-Jacobian (see e.g. Lee, \emph{Riemannian Manifolds}, Lemma 15.19)  
\[
\operatorname{Jac}_{n-1}F(u)=|\det A|\,\|A^{-{\!\top}}u\|_{2}. \tag{9}
\]
Hence  
\[
\begin{aligned}
S(A)&=\int_{S^{\,n-1}}|\det A|\,\|A^{-{\!\top}}u\|_{2}\,d\sigma(u)\\
&=(\det G)^{1/2}\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\bigl(u^{\top}G^{-1}u\bigr)^{1/2}\,d\sigma(u). \tag{10}
\end{aligned}
\]
Put  
\[
I(G):=\int_{S^{\,n-1}}\bigl(u^{\top}G^{-1}u\bigr)^{1/2}\,d\sigma(u),
\qquad\text{so}\qquad S(A)=(\det G)^{1/2}I(G). \tag{11}
\]

Step 3.  Two estimates.

(Jensen). Because $t\mapsto\sqrt t$ is concave,  
\[
I(G)\le |S^{\,n-1}|\,
\sqrt{\frac{1}{|S^{\,n-1}|}\int_{S^{\,n-1}}u^{\top}G^{-1}u\,d\sigma(u)}
=|S^{\,n-1}|\sqrt{\frac{\operatorname{tr}G^{-1}}{n}}. \tag{12}
\]

(Algebraic inequality). We prove  
\[
(\det G)\,\operatorname{tr}G^{-1}\le n. \tag{13}
\]
Let $\lambda_{1},\dots ,\lambda_{n}>0$ be the eigenvalues of $G$.  
From (7) we have the trace constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_{i}=n$.
Define  
\[
\Phi(\lambda):=\log(\det G)+\log(\operatorname{tr}G^{-1})
          =\sum_{i=1}^{n}\log\lambda_{i}+\log\Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\lambda_{i}^{-1}\Bigr).
\]
Maximising $\Phi$ under $\sum_{i}\lambda_{i}=n$ via Lagrange multipliers gives
\(
\lambda_{1}=\dots=\lambda_{n}=1,
\)
so
\(
\max(\det G)\,\operatorname{tr}G^{-1}=n
\)
and (13) follows.  Equality occurs exactly when all $\lambda_{i}=1$ (for $n\ge 3$; in the
two-dimensional case the identity also holds but the uniqueness statement
below will settle equality).

Combining (11), (12) and (13) yields  
\[
S(A)\le |S^{\,n-1}|, \tag{14}
\]
which proves (a).

Step 4.  The equality case.  
Suppose $S(A)=|S^{\,n-1}|$.  Then equality holds in both (12) and (13).

*  Equality in (12) (Jensen) requires the integrand to be $\sigma$-a.e. constant, i.e.  
\[
u^{\top}G^{-1}u=\lambda\quad\forall\,u\in S^{\,n-1}. \tag{15}
\]
Hence $G^{-1}=\lambda I_{n}$ and $G=\lambda^{-1}I_{n}$.

*  Equality in (13) forces $\lambda_{1}=\dots=\lambda_{n}$ (already granted) and, using $\det G=\lambda^{-n}$ together with $\operatorname{tr}G^{-1}=n\lambda$, gives
\(
\lambda^{-n}\cdot n\lambda=n\Rightarrow\lambda=1.
\)
Thus
\[
G=I_{n}. \tag{16}
\]

Therefore $AA^{\top}=I_{n}$, so $A$ is orthogonal.  
Conversely, any orthogonal $A$ is admissible and attains equality in (2).  
This completes part (b). \qed

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
ŌĆó Higher dimension:  The original twoŌĆōdimensional question is replaced by an nŌĆōdimensional one, introducing eigen-values, determinants, traces and integration over S^{nŌłÆ1}.  

ŌĆó Additional constraints:  Instead of four supporting lines we cope with 2n supporting hyperplanes, encoded through the matrix condition diag G = 1.  

ŌĆó Sophisticated structures:  The solution requires differentialŌĆōgeometric computation of the surface-area element of a linear image of the sphere (Step 2), HadamardŌĆÖs determinant inequality, JensenŌĆÖs inequality on the sphere, and eigen-value/minor techniques (Step 5).  

ŌĆó Deeper theory:  Matrix analysis and integration on manifolds replace the elementary trigonometric manipulation sufficient in 2-D.  

ŌĆó Multiple interacting concepts:  Optimisation combines (i) analytic expression of S(A), (ii) convexity/concavity tools, and (iii) linear-algebraic inequalities, all glued together to obtain the sharp bound and the uniqueness clause.

All of these layers render the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the earlier kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n \geq  2 be an integer and put  
  C_n := { x = (x_1,\ldots ,x_n) \in  \mathbb{R}^n : |x_i| \leq  1 for 1 \leq  i \leq  n }  
(the closed unit n-cube centred at the origin).

For every invertible real n \times  n matrix A define the ellipsoid  
  E(A) := { Au : u \in  \mathbb{R}^n and \|u\|_2 \leq  1 }.                               (1)

We call E(A) admissible provided that  
(i)  E(A) \subset  C_n (the ellipsoid is contained in the cube), and  
(ii)  E(A) is tangent to each of the 2n facets x_i = \pm 1 of C_n.

Write S(A) for the (n - 1)-dimensional Hausdorff measure of \partial E(A) (the surface area).

a) Show the isoperimetric-type bound  
  S(A) \leq  S(I_n) = |S^{n-1}|                                             (2)  
for every admissible matrix A.

b) Prove that equality in (2) holds precisely when AA^T = I_n, i.e. when A is orthogonal.  Equivalently, among all ellipsoids that are contained in the cube and tangent to every facet, the Euclidean unit ball is the unique (up to rotation) maximiser of the surface area.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

  G := A A^T  \in  \mathbb{R}^{n\times n},                                                (3)

so that G is symmetric and positive-definite.  The ellipsoid (1) can then be expressed as  

  E(A) = { x \in  \mathbb{R}^n : x^TG^{-1}x \leq  1 }.                                  (4)

Step 1.  Tangency forces unit diagonal.  
Fix k \in  {1,\ldots ,n}.  The extreme value of the k-th coordinate on the ellipsoid is obtained from the optimisation problem  

  maximise e_k^Tx   subject to x^TG^{-1}x = 1.                       (5)

The Lagrange multiplier computation gives the maximiser  
  x* = G e_k / \sqrt{e_k^TG e_k}  
and the maximal value \sqrt{e_k^TG e_k}.  
Because of tangency (ii) this value equals 1, hence  

  e_k^TG e_k = G_{kk} = 1 for every k.                              (6)

Thus  

  diag G = (1,\ldots ,1).                                                   (7)

(Remark.  The inclusion condition (i) is **not** used here; (ii) alone is enough for (7).  Condition (i) will only play a role when we speak about admissible matrices.)

Step 2.  A surface-area formula for E(A).  
Let \sigma  denote the standard surface measure on the unit sphere S^{n-1}.  
The map  

  F : S^{n-1} \to  \partial E(A), F(u)=Au,                                    (8)

is a smooth parametrisation.  For each u \in  S^{n-1} one has (see e.g. Lee, *Riemannian Manifolds*, Lemma 15.19)  

  Jac_{n-1}F(u)=det A\cdot \|A^{-^T}u\|_2.                                (9)

Hence  

  S(A)=\int _{S^{n-1}}det A\cdot \|A^{-^T}u\|_2 d\sigma (u)  
         =(det G)^{1/2} \int _{S^{n-1}}(u^TG^{-1}u)^{1/2}d\sigma (u).            (10)

Introduce  

  I(G):=\int _{S^{n-1}}(u^TG^{-1}u)^{1/2} d\sigma (u), so S(A)=(det G)^{1/2}I(G). (11)

Step 3.  First inequalities: Hadamard and Jensen.  
Because of (7) Hadamard's determinant bound gives  

  det G \leq  1,                                                       (12)

with equality iff G = I_n.  

For the integral I(G) note that t \mapsto  \sqrt{t} is concave.  Applying Jensen to the probability measure \sigma /|S^{n-1}| we obtain  

  I(G) \leq  |S^{n-1}|\cdot \sqrt{\langle u^TG^{-1}u\rangle },                               (13)

where \langle \cdot \rangle  denotes averaging over S^{n-1}.  The standard identity \langle u^TMu\rangle  = (tr M)/n yields  

  I(G) \leq  |S^{n-1}|\cdot \sqrt{(tr G^{-1})/n}.                              (14)

Combining (11), (12) and (14) we obtain  

  S(A) \leq  |S^{n-1}|\cdot \sqrt{(det G)(tr G^{-1})/n}.                      (15)

Thus it suffices to prove the purely algebraic estimate  

  (det G)(tr G^{-1}) \leq  n.                                          (16)

Step 4.  Rewriting (det G)(tr G^{-1}).  
For a symmetric positive-definite matrix G let G^{ii} denote the determinant of the principal (n-1) \times  (n-1) minor obtained by deleting the i-th row and column.  Cramer's rule gives  

  (G^{-1})_{ii} = G^{ii}/det G.                                    (17)

Hence  

  (det G)(tr G^{-1}) = \sum _{i=1}^{n}G^{ii}.                         (18)

Step 5.  Bounding every principal minor.  
Because each reduced matrix still has diagonal entries equal to 1, Hadamard's inequality implies  

  G^{ii} \leq  1 for all i.                                            (19)

Step 6.  Completion of the estimate and of part (a).  
Summing (19) and using (18) yields (16).  Substituting (16) back into (15) proves  

  S(A) \leq  |S^{n-1}| = S(I_n),                                      (20)

establishing (2).

Step 7.  The equality case.  
Suppose S(A)=|S^{n-1}|.  Equality must then occur in every intermediate inequality:

* From (12) we need det G = 1, whence by Hadamard G = I_n.  
* Conversely, if G = I_n then (11) immediately gives S(A)=|S^{n-1}|.

Thus equality in (2) is equivalent to G = I_n.  But G = I_n means  

  AA^T = I_n \Leftrightarrow  A \in  O(n).                                          (21)

Any orthogonal matrix clearly satisfies (i) and (ii): E(A) is the Euclidean unit ball, which is contained in the cube and touches every facet at the points \pm e_1,\ldots ,\pm e_n.  Hence the Euclidean unit ball is the unique admissible ellipsoid of maximal surface area, and equality holds precisely for orthogonal A. \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
ŌĆó Higher dimension:  The original twoŌĆōdimensional question is replaced by an nŌĆōdimensional one, introducing eigen-values, determinants, traces and integration over S^{nŌłÆ1}.  

ŌĆó Additional constraints:  Instead of four supporting lines we cope with 2n supporting hyperplanes, encoded through the matrix condition diag G = 1.  

ŌĆó Sophisticated structures:  The solution requires differentialŌĆōgeometric computation of the surface-area element of a linear image of the sphere (Step 2), HadamardŌĆÖs determinant inequality, JensenŌĆÖs inequality on the sphere, and eigen-value/minor techniques (Step 5).  

ŌĆó Deeper theory:  Matrix analysis and integration on manifolds replace the elementary trigonometric manipulation sufficient in 2-D.  

ŌĆó Multiple interacting concepts:  Optimisation combines (i) analytic expression of S(A), (ii) convexity/concavity tools, and (iii) linear-algebraic inequalities, all glued together to obtain the sharp bound and the uniqueness clause.

All of these layers render the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the earlier kernel variant.
